pathophysiology_summary: ACE inhibitors block kininase II (ACE), reducing bradykinin breakdown. Accumulated bradykinin increases vascular permeability in the deep dermis and subcutaneous tissues, producing non-pitting, non-pruritic angioedema with a predilection for the lips, face, tongue and — dangerously — the larynx. Because the mechanism is bradykinin-mediated rather than mast-cell/histamine-mediated, urticaria and pruritus are characteristically absent, and adrenaline, antihistamines and corticosteroids are of limited or no benefit. Onset can occur anywhere from hours to years after starting the drug (here 10 days). Definitive management is immediate and permanent cessation of the ACE inhibitor plus vigilant airway monitoring; refractory or airway-threatening cases may require icatibant, C1-esterase-inhibitor concentrate or fresh frozen plasma in hospital. The immediate life threat is progression to laryngeal oedema and airway obstruction.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden, rapidly progressive deep swelling of the lips and face.
Airway-region swelling always mandates screening for anaphylaxis; must be actively excluded before it is set aside.
Facial swelling; less likely given the hyper-acute onset over hours.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, anaphylaxis is the important-not-to-miss diagnosis to screen for; the bradykinin pattern is not yet established.

differential.evidence_basis: Deep, tense, non-pruritic, non-urticarial swelling with a recently commenced ACE inhibitor.
Made less likely by the absence of urticaria, pruritus and any identified allergen exposure.
No pruritus, weeping or superficial epidermal change; the swelling is deep and subcutaneous.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The '-pril' history plus the non-pruritic, urticaria-free deep swelling shifts the leading diagnosis to bradykinin-mediated ACEi angioedema.

differential.evidence_basis: Negative anaphylaxis screen (no generalised urticaria, wheeze, hypotension or GI symptoms) with a positive drug trigger confirms the bradykinin pattern.
Excluded by absence of systemic involvement — no widespread urticaria, wheeze, hypotension or GI symptoms.
First presentation and clear ACEi trigger make this less likely, but it should be considered if angioedema recurs after ACEi cessation (check C4 / C1-INH).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Anaphylaxis is now excluded on the negative systemic screen; the residual airway risk is due to the bradykinin angioedema itself, not to allergy.

differential.diagnosis: ACE inhibitor-induced (bradykinin) angioedema of the face and lips, airway currently patent
differential.evidence_basis: Deep non-pruritic non-urticarial airway-region angioedema, recent ACE inhibitor, negative anaphylaxis screen; airway clear now but unexcludable remotely and can progress.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-state is recognition of ACEi angioedema, immediate drug cessation, urgent airway-capable in-person assessment, and a low-threshold 000 airway safety-net — not reliance on allergy treatment.

red_flags.description: Angioedema of the airway region (lips/face, with potential tongue/pharyngeal/laryngeal spread) — the airway is the immediate life threat and cannot be examined via telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: Laryngeal oedema can cause fatal airway obstruction; a currently clear voice does not exclude developing internal swelling.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected progression to airway obstruction and asphyxiation, potentially during transit to a distant hospital.

red_flags.description: Bradykinin-mediated mechanism from a recently commenced ACE inhibitor — poorly responsive to adrenaline/antihistamines/steroids and will persist or recur unless the drug is stopped
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifying and permanently ceasing the ACE inhibitor is the pivotal treatment; relying on allergy therapy is both ineffective and delays definitive action.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued ACE inhibitor exposure with ongoing or recurrent, potentially airway-threatening angioedema.

red_flags.description: Rapid progression of swelling over approximately 2 hours
red_flags.clinical_significance: The rate of progression predicts the risk of imminent airway involvement and mandates urgent, not routine, assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed escalation while swelling advances toward the airway.

red_flags.description: Geographically remote from definitive airway care (about 30 minutes from the nearest hospital)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A long transit time lowers the threshold for early 000 activation and mandates ambulance (not self-transport) if the airway deteriorates.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Airway decompensation en route without paramedic airway support.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the facial swelling as an allergic reaction and relying on adrenaline, antihistamines and corticosteroids — which are of limited benefit in bradykinin-mediated angioedema — while missing the ACE inhibitor cause.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a currently clear airway
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured that the patient is speaking in full sentences and under-triaging, when bradykinin angioedema of the airway region can progress over the following hours.
Failing to ask about a recently started '-pril' (ACE inhibitor) and therefore not identifying or ceasing the causative drug.
Attempting to confirm airway safety remotely; the oropharynx and larynx cannot be examined by chat or video, so airway involvement must be treated as unexcludable.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct oropharyngeal / laryngeal examination for uvular, pharyngeal or laryngeal oedema
Auscultation for stridor or air entry
Objective blood pressure and heart rate without a home device
Whether the swelling is currently progressing internally toward the airway
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Cease the ACE inhibitor immediately; arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment where the airway can be examined; give explicit, mandatory safety-netting to call 000 immediately for any voice change, throat tightness, difficulty swallowing saliva, tongue swelling or noisy/difficult breathing (ambulance, not self-transport).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the bradykinin/ACE-inhibitor pattern is recognisable, but the real danger is under-action: mistaking it for simple allergy, or being reassured by a currently clear airway.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG): angioedema / ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Cease the ACE inhibitor permanently; ACEi angioedema is bradykinin-mediated and responds poorly to adrenaline/antihistamines/corticosteroids; monitor and protect the airway; avoid all ACE inhibitors lifelong and use ARBs only with caution.
Angioedema without urticaria should prompt consideration of bradykinin-mediated causes (ACE inhibitors, hereditary/acquired C1-INH deficiency); histamine-directed therapy is frequently ineffective.
